agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental change to the core domain model of a delivery platform, affecting data structures, partner integrations, delivery workflows, and the meaning of 'delivery completion' itself.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Delivery Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods moving from origin to one or more delivery points. Formerly assumed a single delivery endpoint; now represents a delivery job that may involve multiple stops."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A named party who receives part or all of a Shipment at a specific address, with a phone number for contact and individual delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-level configuration such as time windows, safe-place instructions, signature requirements, or contactless delivery. New concept introduced with multi-recipient support."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"The first or default Recipient on a Shipment. Critical for backward compatibility — the single Recipient on legacy Shipments becomes the Primary Recipient."},{"term":"Delivery Completion","definition":"The state indicating a Shipment has been fully delivered. Under the new model, requires a clear rule: is a Shipment complete when ALL recipients are served, or when ANY is served?"},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integrations that consume Shipment data in the current single-recipient format. Must be preserved or versioned during migration."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment without a Recipient has no delivery destination and is physically meaningless — it represents corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"Given a Shipment, when querying its Recipients, then the count is always >= 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment must have a name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These three fields are the minimum required to execute a physical delivery: who, where, and how to contact. Without any one of them, the courier cannot complete the delivery.","testable_assertion":"Given a Recipient being added to a Shipment, when any of name, address, or phone is missing, then the operation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment must have a unique address. No two Recipients on the same Shipment may share an identical address.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Two recipients at the same address on the same shipment is a data integrity issue — they should be merged into one Recipient with multiple items, or modeled differently.","testable_assertion":"Given a Shipment with Recipient at address A, when adding another Recipient with address A, then the operation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Delivery preferences are optional per Recipient and default to platform standards if not specified.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Defaults are a policy decision that can change. The business may decide to require preferences in the future.","testable_assertion":"Given a Recipient with no explicit delivery preferences, when the Shipment is dispatched, then platform default preferences apply."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Legacy single-recipient Shipments must be migrated to the multi-recipient model with the existing Recipient as the sole (and primary) Recipient, with no data loss.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Data integrity for 2 million existing records. Loss or corruption of recipient data breaks downstream billing, tracking, and compliance.","testable_assertion":"Given a legacy Shipment with Recipient R, when migration completes, then the Shipment has exactly one Recipient R with all original fields preserved and marked as primary."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"External partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data in the original single-recipient format until partners explicitly migrate to a new API version.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a compatibility policy. Partners can be migrated on a timeline, but the business decides when to deprecate.","testable_assertion":"Given a multi-recipient Shipment, when partner API sends data in v1 format, then only the primary Recipient is included in the payload."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A Shipment's completion status requires ALL Recipients to have received delivery (or have a terminal status such as refused/undeliverable).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partial delivery of a multi-recipient shipment means the job is not done. The shipment lifecycle cannot close while Recipients remain in a non-terminal state — this would corrupt tracking and billing.","testable_assertion":"Given a Shipment with Recipients A, B, and C, when A and B are delivered but C has no terminal status, then the Shipment status is not 'Completed'."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"the creator specifies one or more Recipients each with name, address, and phone","then":"the Shipment is created successfully with all Recipients persisted and the first Recipient marked as primary"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"no Recipients are specified","then":"creation is rejected with a validation error stating at least one Recipient is required"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An existing Shipment with one Recipient","when":"a second Recipient is added with valid name, address, and phone","then":"the Shipment now has two Recipients and both are independently trackable"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment with Recipients A and B","when":"Recipient A is delivered and Recipient B is still pending","then":"the Shipment status remains 'In Progress' (not 'Completed')"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A legacy single-recipient Shipment in the database","when":"the data migration runs","then":"the Shipment has exactly one Recipient with all original fields intact and delivery preferences set to platform defaults"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"a partner API request is made using the v1 (legacy) format","then":"the response contains only the primary Recipient's data in the original schema shape"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A Shipment with two Recipients at the same address","when":"the second Recipient is being added","then":"the operation is rejected with a duplicate-address validation error"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A Recipient on a Shipment","when":"no delivery preferences are provided","then":"platform default delivery preferences are applied automatically"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Removing the last Recipient from a Shipment","expected_behavior":"Operation must be rejected. A Shipment must always have >= 1 Recipient (BR-1). If business intent is cancellation, a separate cancellation workflow should be used."},{"scenario":"Multi-recipient Shipment where one Recipient refuses delivery","expected_behavior":"That Recipient moves to terminal status 'Refused'. The Shipment remains in progress until all other Recipients reach a terminal status. Return/refund logic applies only to the refused portion."},{"scenario":"Partner API webhook fires for a multi-recipient Shipment","expected_behavior":"If partner is on v1 API, webhook payload contains only the primary Recipient. If partner has migrated to v2, webhook contains all Recipients. Versioning must be explicit per partner."},{"scenario":"Editing a Recipient's address on an in-transit Shipment","expected_behavior":"Must trigger re-routing for the courier. If the Shipment is already past the point of diversion for that Recipient, the edit should be rejected or flagged for manual intervention."},{"scenario":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment in different cities","expected_behavior":"This implies a split-shipment or multi-leg delivery. The system must determine whether this is supported or whether Recipients must be within the same delivery zone. This needs product clarification."},{"scenario":"Migration of a legacy Shipment that is currently in-transit","expected_behavior":"Migration must be safe for in-flight Shipments. The single Recipient becomes the primary Recipient with no change to courier instructions or tracking state."},{"scenario":"Bulk creation of Shipments with 50+ Recipients each","expected_behavior":"System must define an upper bound for Recipients per Shipment. Unbounded lists create performance, UX, and operational risks. A reasonable cap (e.g., 10-20) should be set as policy."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-recipient shipments can increase revenue per shipment by consolidating deliveries. However, the migration carries cost: engineering effort, partner re-integration, and risk of data corruption affecting 2M records. Incorrect completion logic could lead to premature billing or under-billing.","operational":"Courier dispatch and route optimization must account for multiple stops per shipment. This changes the delivery workflow fundamentally — drivers need updated instructions, and operations dashboards must show per-recipient status within a shipment. Support teams need training on the new model.","user":"Recipients gain individual delivery preferences, improving satisfaction. However, tracking UX must evolve — a single tracking number now maps to multiple deliveries, which could confuse end users if not clearly communicated.","risk":"HIGH. Data migration of 2M records is the primary risk. Partner API backward compatibility is the secondary risk — breaking partner integrations could cause contractual violations. Domain model change affects every layer: persistence, API, events, reporting. Incorrect rollout could corrupt shipment state.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Must handle multi-stop deliveries per shipment. Route optimization changes. Need updated mobile app UI showing per-recipient delivery status."},{"group":"Recipients/End Users","impact":"Gain delivery preferences but tracking experience changes. A single tracking ID now represents multiple deliveries — UX must clearly show per-recipient status."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Partners on v1 API must not break. New v2 API must be offered with clear migration timeline and documentation."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Dispatch logic must account for multi-recipient routing. Dashboards and reporting must show per-recipient delivery status within a shipment."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Billing model may need to change — is pricing per shipment or per recipient? Completion triggers for invoicing must align with the new all-recipients-delivered rule."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Support agents need visibility into per-recipient status. Escalation paths change when only some recipients on a shipment have issues."}]},"confidence":{"level":55,"reasoning":"The product team's request is clear directionally but leaves several critical domain questions unanswered: completion semantics, geographic constraints on recipients, upper bounds, billing model changes, and the exact definition of 'delivery preferences.' The existing model and migration scope are well-understood, but the target state has ambiguities that could lead to incorrect implementation if assumed.","high_confidence_areas":["The invariant that a Shipment must have >= 1 Recipient","Legacy data migration must preserve all existing Recipient data","Partner API backward compatibility is required","Each Recipient must retain name, address, phone as mandatory fields"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Recipients on the same Shipment can be in different cities/zones — this changes the model from 'multi-recipient' to 'multi-leg'","Upper bound on Recipients per Shipment — unbounded is operationally dangerous","Exact schema and allowed values for 'delivery preferences' — vaguely defined","Whether billing changes from per-shipment to per-recipient pricing","Whether 'primary Recipient' is an explicit domain concept or just a migration artifact"],"assumptions":["Shipment completion requires ALL recipients to reach terminal status (not just one)","The first/only Recipient on legacy Shipments becomes the 'primary' Recipient","Partner APIs will be versioned (v1 for backward compat, v2 for multi-recipient)","Delivery preferences are optional and have platform defaults","Recipients on the same Shipment must have unique addresses"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is introduced without definition. What options are included? Time windows? Signature requirements? Safe-place instructions? Contactless delivery? The implementation cannot proceed without a concrete schema for this new concept.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Team","Engineering","Recipients"],"options":["Define a minimal set (time window + signature + safe place) and expand later","Let Recipients free-text their preferences","Define a full preference taxonomy upfront"],"recommendation":"Define a minimal, enumerated set of delivery preferences for v1 (e.g., time_window, requires_signature, safe_place_instructions, contactless). Expand iteratively based on user feedback."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Can Recipients on the same Shipment be in different cities or delivery zones? This fundamentally changes whether this is a 'multi-drop single route' or a 'multi-leg shipment' — two very different domain concepts with different operational, pricing, and courier implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Couriers","Finance/Billing"],"options":["Restrict Recipients to the same delivery zone (multi-drop)","Allow any addresses (multi-leg, requires shipment splitting)","Allow any addresses but auto-split into sub-shipments behind the scenes"],"recommendation":"Start with same-zone restriction (multi-drop). Multi-leg is a fundamentally different domain concept and should be a separate initiative."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No upper bound defined for Recipients per Shipment. An unbounded list creates performance risk in routing, UX complexity in tracking, and operational burden for couriers.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Couriers","Operations"],"options":["Set a hard cap (e.g., 10 Recipients per Shipment)","Set a soft cap with override capability for enterprise accounts","No cap — handle operationally"],"recommendation":"Set a hard cap of 10 Recipients per Shipment for v1. This covers 99% of use cases while protecting system performance and courier experience."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Shipment completion semantics are not specified. Does a multi-recipient Shipment complete when ALL Recipients are served, or when ANY is served? This affects billing triggers, SLA calculations, and reporting.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance/Billing","Operations","Customer Support"],"options":["ALL Recipients must reach terminal status for Shipment completion","ANY Recipient delivery completes the Shipment","Per-recipient completion with Shipment-level rollup status"],"recommendation":"ALL Recipients must reach terminal status (delivered, refused, or undeliverable). This is the only interpretation consistent with the physical reality of a delivery job."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking: Let me analyze this domain model evolution from single-recipient to multi-recipient shipments.

This is a fundamental change to the core domain model of a delivery platform, affecting data structures, partner integrations, delivery workflows, and the meaning of 'delivery completion' itself.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Delivery Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods moving from origin to one or more delivery points.
